title: Strings
description: String manipulation is at the core of many interview problems. Master iteration, slicing, splitting, character codes, and the critical concept of string immutability in JavaScript.

sections.title: Iterating over characters
sections.explanation: JavaScript strings are iterable. Use `for...of` to iterate characters or spread into an array with `[...str]`. Index access with `str[i]` works but `str.charAt(i)` is the classic alternative. For interview problems involving character frequency or palindromes, iteration is the first step.

exercise.prompt: Given string `s = "abcd"`, iterate with for...of and build a reversed string. Print the reversed string. Then print the length of the original.

validation.message: Use a for...of loop to iterate the string characters

exercise.hint: Use 'of' to iterate and prepend each char: reversed = ch + reversed.

sections.title: substring and slice
sections.explanation: Use `str.slice(start, end)` to extract substrings — it supports negative indices. `str.substring(start, end)` is similar but swaps arguments if start > end and doesn't support negatives. In interviews, slice is generally preferred for consistency with array operations.
sections.example:
const s = "interview";
console.log(s.slice(0, 5));   // "inter"
console.log(s.slice(5));      // "view"
console.log(s.slice(-4));     // "view"
console.log(s.substring(0, 5)); // "inter"

exercise.prompt: Given `s = "javascript"`, extract and print: the first 4 characters using slice, the last 6 characters using slice with a negative index, and everything from index 4 onward.

validation.message: First 4 chars are 'java', last 6 are 'script', from index 4 is 'script'
Use the slice method for all extractions

exercise.hint: slice(0, 4) for first 4, slice(-6) for last 6, slice(4) for index 4 onward.

sections.title: split and join
sections.explanation: split() converts a string to an array, join() converts an array back to a string. This pattern is essential because JavaScript strings are immutable — to rearrange characters, split into an array, manipulate, then join back. Interviewers love problems that require this roundtrip.

exercise.prompt: Given `sentence = "the quick brown fox"`, split by spaces into an array, print the number of words, then join with hyphens and print the result.

validation.message: 4 words when split by space, joined by hyphens
Use split to break and join to reassemble the string

sections.title: charCodeAt and fromCharCode
sections.explanation: charCodeAt(i) returns the Unicode value of a character — essential for problems involving character math (e.g., mapping 'a' to 0 for frequency arrays). String.fromCharCode() converts back. Know that 'a' is 97 and 'A' is 65.
sections.example:
console.log("a".charCodeAt(0));  // 97
console.log("z".charCodeAt(0));  // 122
console.log("A".charCodeAt(0));  // 65

// Character arithmetic
const idx = "c".charCodeAt(0) - "a".charCodeAt(0); // 2
console.log(String.fromCharCode(97)); // 'a'

exercise.prompt: Print the char code of 'a', compute the alphabet index of 'e' (e - a), and convert char code 122 back to a character. Print each on its own line.

validation.message: char code of 'a' is 97, 'e'-'a' is 4, char code 122 is 'z'
Use charCodeAt for encoding and fromCharCode for decoding

exercise.hint: Subtract 'a'.charCodeAt(0) to get alphabet index. fromCharCode(122) is 'z'.

sections.title: String immutability
sections.explanation: JavaScript strings are immutable — you cannot change a character in place. Every string operation returns a new string. For interview problems needing character manipulation, convert to an array first, modify, then join. This is a critical performance consideration for building strings in loops.

exercise.prompt: Given `s = "abcde"`, convert to an array, swap the first and last characters, join back, and print the result.

validation.message: Use split/join to work around string immutability

exercise.hint: chars.length - 1 for the last index, and temp holds the original first character.
